title: PowerShell PnP.PowerShell - SharePoint CRUD, Authentication, And Batching
applies_to: SharePoint list item create, read, update, delete from PowerShell
File uploads to SharePoint document libraries
Bulk data staging with batch operations
App-only or Managed Identity SharePoint automation

confirmed_facts.fact: PnP.PowerShell is the community-maintained cross-platform PowerShell module for SharePoint Online built on PnP Core SDK. Install: `Install-Module PnP.PowerShell -Scope CurrentUser`. Version 2.x requires PowerShell 7.2 or later. Version 1.x supports both Windows PowerShell 5.1 and PowerShell 7.

Certificate-based app-only connection (production recommended): `Connect-PnPOnline -Url "https://tenant.sharepoint.com/sites/sitename" -ClientId $appId -Tenant $tenantId -CertificatePath $certPath -CertificatePassword (ConvertTo-SecureString $certPass -AsPlainText -Force)`. The app registration requires Sites.Selected or Sites.FullControl.All Graph permission.

Client secret app-only connection (legacy, being deprecated by Microsoft): `Connect-PnPOnline -Url $siteUrl -ClientId $appId -ClientSecret $secret`. This uses the Azure ACS (Access Control Service) model. Microsoft recommends migrating to certificate or Managed Identity auth.

Managed Identity connection (Azure Automation, Azure Functions): `Connect-PnPOnline -Url $siteUrl -ManagedIdentity`. The Managed Identity must be granted SharePoint site permissions via `Set-PnPTenantSite` or via Microsoft Graph. No credential management required.

Add a list item: `Add-PnPListItem -List "ListName" -Values @{ "Title" = "value"; "InternalFieldName" = "value" }`. Returns a Microsoft.SharePoint.Client.ListItem object containing the new item's `Id` property. Values hashtable uses internal column names.

Update a list item: `Set-PnPListItem -List "ListName" -Identity $itemId -Values @{ "InternalFieldName" = "newvalue" }`. The -Identity parameter accepts an integer item ID or a ListItem object returned from Get-PnPListItem or Add-PnPListItem.

Retrieve list items with CAML filtering (efficient for large lists): `Get-PnPListItem -List "ListName" -Query "<View><Query><Where><Eq><FieldRef Name='SourceKey'/><Value Type='Text'>$key</Value></Eq></Where></Query></View>"`. CAML filtering happens server-side and is much faster than client-side filtering for upsert lookups.

Retrieve all items from a list: `Get-PnPListItem -List "ListName" -PageSize 500`. Use -PageSize to control server-side pagination batch size. Default is 100. PnP automatically follows pages until all items are returned.

Batch operations for bulk writes: `$batch = New-PnPBatch; foreach ($row in $data) { Add-PnPListItem -List "ListName" -Values $row -Batch $batch }; Invoke-PnPBatch -Batch $batch`. Batches up to 100 operations per Invoke-PnPBatch call for optimal performance. Always split large datasets into chunks of 100.

Discover column internal names: `Get-PnPField -List "ListName" | Select-Object Title, InternalName, TypeAsString`. The InternalName is required for all Values hashtables. Display names are not accepted. Columns with spaces in display names often have underscores in internal names.

Upload a file to a document library: `Add-PnPFile -Path $localFilePath -Folder "Shared Documents/Reports"`. Returns a Microsoft.SharePoint.Client.File object. For files larger than 10MB, use chunked upload: `Add-PnPFile -Path $localFilePath -Folder $folder -Chunked -ChunkSize 10` (ChunkSize in MB).
Delete a list item: `Remove-PnPListItem -List "ListName" -Identity $itemId -Force`. The -Force flag suppresses the confirmation prompt, required for unattended scripts.

implementation_notes: PnP.PowerShell is generally preferred over Microsoft.Graph SDK for SharePoint-heavy workloads due to simpler CAML-based filtering, built-in batching, and higher-level abstraction over Graph REST calls.
Run a one-time discovery of list internal names using Get-PnPField and cache results. Internal names never change even if display names are renamed.
For upsert logic: query by source key with CAML, check result count, then call Add-PnPListItem or Set-PnPListItem accordingly. This avoids duplicates on repeated script runs.
Split bulk inserts into batches of 100: `$chunks = [System.Linq.Enumerable]::Chunk($data, 100)`. This avoids throttling and ensures each batch completes within SharePoint's request timeout.
Always call `Disconnect-PnPOnline` at the end of scripts to release the connection context and avoid token leakage in long-running processes.
caveats: PnP.PowerShell 2.x requires PowerShell 7.2+. Scripts running on Windows PowerShell 5.1 must use PnP.PowerShell 1.x, which has a slightly different API surface.
Client secret (ACS) auth is being phased out by Microsoft. New implementations should use certificate-based or Managed Identity auth.
Managed Identity must be explicitly granted site-level permissions; granting SharePoint admin rights does not automatically flow to specific sites.
CAML queries are case-sensitive on field reference names. Use the exact InternalName value from Get-PnPField.
Batch operations with Set-PnPListItem (update) in a batch context may not be supported in all PnP versions; verify batch support for updates vs. adds.
SharePoint Online enforces resource throttling. On 429 responses, PnP.PowerShell automatically retries. For custom HTTP calls outside PnP, implement explicit retry logic.
